policy_statement: Implement COVID-19 recovery coastal/marine actions including 15,000 hectares of mangrove planting and 50 hectares of coral reef restoration in Bali in 2020.
mechanism_description: The programme implements mangrove planting and coral reef restoration to enhance coastal ecosystems, increasing carbon sequestration and strengthening coastal resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem restoration → ↑ ecosystem carbon stocks + ↑ natural coastal protection → ↓ net atmospheric CO₂ + ↓ climate hazard impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Implement coastal ecosystem restoration (e.g., mangrove planting, coral reef rehabilitation) and associated management/protection that enables ecosystems to recover and persist.  
(2) Mediating processes: Mangroves accumulate biomass and build carbon-rich soils/sediments (“blue carbon” sequestration and storage); reefs/mangroves dissipate wave energy, stabilize shorelines, and reduce erosion and storm-surge penetration.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcomes: Increased long-term carbon sequestration/avoided carbon loss (mitigation) and reduced exposure/vulnerability of coastal communities and assets to sea-level rise, storms, and coastal flooding/erosion (adaptation).
dominant_pathway_test: Restoring mangroves/reefs most importantly improves ecosystem function to increase carbon storage (blue carbon) while also buffering coasts from storms and erosion.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem enhancement is the core lever; climate benefits arise via increased carbon sinks (blue carbon) and reduced coastal hazard impacts through natural wave attenuation and shoreline stabilization.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism has both mitigation and adaptation outcomes, the shared, defining approach is restoring/protecting nature to deliver climate services—i.e., a Nature-Based Solution—rather than an engineered emissions-reduction measure (Mitigation) or purely exposure-reduction infrastructure (Adaptation) or efficiency improvement (Resource Efficiency).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program were only coastal protection via hard infrastructure, it would shift toward Adaptation; if it were exclusively carbon-credit forestry with no resilience intent, it could be framed as Mitigation—but this canonical mechanism explicitly bundles ecosystem restoration with carbon sequestration and coastal resilience.  
4) Applies to all instances: Any policy that fits “coastal ecosystem restoration → carbon sequestration and coastal resilience” necessarily uses ecosystem restoration as the primary intervention, so Nature-Based Solutions remains the correct primary category across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Specific subtypes (e.g., mitigation-focused blue carbon vs adaptation-focused coastal protection) depend on policy-level evidence and are deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some coastal ecosystem actions (e.g., coral restoration) have limited direct carbon sequestration compared with mangroves; however, they strongly support coastal resilience and can protect adjacent blue-carbon habitats. Classification remains NBS because the mechanism is fundamentally ecosystem-based with climate services (sink + protection).
key_indicators: "COVID-19 recovery programme"; "15 thousands hectare mangrove planting"; "50 hectare coral reef restoration"; "coastal and marine based activities"; "marine tourism area in Bali"
co_benefits: Economic recovery/jobs; support for marine tourism; biodiversity and fisheries habitat improvement; water quality improvement
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy or target-setting because it specifies hectare amounts, but the wording emphasizes implemented activities (planting/restoration), so it is treated as direct investment/action.
